title: Lesson 14 — The Difficult Text

words.example: A lucid explanation even a beginner could follow.
A cogent argument that changed my mind on the spot.

words.definition: logical, consistent, and easy to follow
words.example: A coherent essay with one clear line of thought.

words.definition: clearly expressed and easy to understand
words.example: Her perspicuous prose never needed a second reading.
An abstruse theorem that few in the room could grasp.

words.definition: understood by only a small group with special knowledge
words.example: Esoteric jargon baffled everyone new to the field.

words.definition: understood by few; mysterious or secret
words.example: The arcane rules of the ancient game confused newcomers.

words.definition: hard to understand; not clear or transparent in meaning
words.example: The contract's opaque clauses hid all the extra fees.

words.definition: open to more than one interpretation; unclear
words.example: An ambiguous reply that could mean yes or no.

words.definition: having a hidden or obscure meaning
words.example: A cryptic note with no signature and no date.
A nebulous plan with no real steps behind it.

words.definition: annoyingly slow to understand; lacking sharpness of mind
words.example: He was too obtuse to take the obvious hint.

story: The visiting scholar's first lecture was a model of clarity — [[lucid|lucid]], [[cogent|cogent]], [[coherent|coherent]], and so [[perspicuous|perspicuous]] that even freshmen took clean notes. His new book was the opposite. It was [[abstruse|abstruse]] and [[esoteric|esoteric]], stuffed with [[arcane|arcane]] references and [[opaque|opaque]] proofs. Whole chapters read as [[ambiguous|ambiguous]], others openly [[cryptic|cryptic]], and the conclusion stayed maddeningly [[nebulous|nebulous]]. A reviewer — either very honest or slightly [[obtuse|obtuse]] — finally asked why a man who could speak so plainly chose to write so darkly.
